researchQuestionsToAnalyses.rq: Are yes/no positive screens more common in OCD than in healthy controls?
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.analysis: Chi-square (chi-square count test) for each behaviour in Table 2, plus a check for "at least one behavioural addiction" overall.
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.why: Chi-square works on counts. It checks whether the split of positive vs not-positive is different across the two groups. Small p-value means the gap is unlikely to be random.

researchQuestionsToAnalyses.rq: If we compare raw severity scores (not just yes/no), does OCD still differ from controls?
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.analysis: Mann-Whitney U (rank comparison test) for Table 3 scores, because score shape was skewed (not bell-shaped).
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.why: When scores are very uneven, a classic t-test can mislead. Mann-Whitney ranks everyone and asks whether one group usually sits higher.

researchQuestionsToAnalyses.rq: Inside the OCD group, does screen-positive vs screen-negative differ on age, sex, severity, impulsivity, and comorbidity?
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.analysis: Chi-square for count rows and t-tests for continuous rows in Table 4.
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.why: There are two OCD subgroups. Count data uses chi-square; number averages use t-tests where the authors used parametric testing.

researchQuestionsToAnalyses.rq: Do the three groups differ on BIS-11 total impulsivity?
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.analysis: One-way ANOVA (three-group average comparison) with Figure 1 means ± SD and p < 0.001.
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.why: ANOVA checks three groups in one test. Figure 1 shows a step-up pattern: HC, then OCD, then OCD+BA.

researchQuestionsToAnalyses.rq: Across all people together, do higher impulsivity scores move with higher behaviour severity scores?
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.analysis: Bivariate correlation (move-together check) between BIS-11 total and each BA score, excluding very rare behaviours.
researchQuestionsToAnalyses.why: Correlation asks whether two number scales rise and fall together. It shows association, not cause.

spssWorkflow.step: 1. Check score shape first (normal vs skewed)
spssWorkflow.detail: The paper says behavioural severity scores were skewed, so it used Mann-Whitney for OCD vs control in Table 3. It does not list every normality check detail. Learning point: check score shape before picking a test.
In SPSS, use Crosstabs and request chi-square. Put group (OCD/control) on one side and yes/no caseness on the other. This reproduces Table 2.

spssWorkflow.step: 3. Compare skewed severity scores between two groups
spssWorkflow.detail: Use Mann-Whitney U for each continuous score in OCD vs controls. This is the Table 3 logic.

spssWorkflow.step: 4. Split OCD into screen-positive and screen-negative
spssWorkflow.detail: Use t-tests for continuous variables and chi-square for category/count variables. This matches Table 4.

spssWorkflow.step: 5. Compare BIS total across three groups
spssWorkflow.detail: Use one-way ANOVA with HC, OCD, and OCD+BA. Figure 1 shows means ± SD. The extracted text does not report full post-hoc details.
Pair BIS total with each behavioural score, excluding very rare behaviours. Remember: correlation is association, not cause.

mannWhitneyVsTtest.thisStudy: Mann-Whitney was used for OCD vs control severity because the score shapes were skewed (not bell-shaped).
mannWhitneyVsTtest.tTestWouldNeed: A t-test usually fits better when score shapes are fairly normal and group spread is reasonably similar.
mannWhitneyVsTtest.ruleOfThumb: The same paper can mix tools: Mann-Whitney for skewed comparisons and t-test where assumptions look acceptable.
